steps.title: Identify what punctuation is needed
steps.content: The blank sits between “a” and the relative clause “who was not the first to construct the device.” The phrase in this case, Henry Cavendish is a non-restrictive appositive—extra identifying information that can be removed without changing the sentence's meaning. Non-restrictive appositives require matching delimiters on both sides (both commas, both parentheses, or both dashes).

steps.title: Test each choice against the matching-delimiter rule
steps.content:
• Choice A — natural philosopher: in this case, Henry Cavendish,: Uses a colon to open and a comma to close—mismatched. A colon also cannot set off a mid-sentence appositive this way. ✗
  • Choice B — natural philosopher—in this case, Henry Cavendish,: Opens with a dash but closes with a comma—mismatched delimiters. ✗
  • Choice C — natural philosopher in this case, Henry Cavendish,: No opening delimiter at all; the appositive runs into the noun without any separation. ✗
  • Choice D — natural philosopher—in this case, Henry Cavendish—: Opens and closes with dashes, correctly bracketing the non-restrictive appositive on both sides. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
